name: Ruby
summary: Dynamic object-oriented language with lightweight runtime loading: require/load executes a file, classes and modules are open and reopenable, and monkey-patching is normal; module boundaries function more as convention than hard encapsulation, weakening static analyzability in exchange for REPL-friendly flexibility.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Ruby is a dynamic object-oriented language designed by Yukihiro Matsumoto, with lightweight runtime loading and culturally permissive open-class semantics. The reference implementation is MRI/CRuby; alternatives include JRuby and TruffleRuby. This record covers the language-level module-system surface.

sections.title: require / load and soft module boundaries
sections.content:
Ruby's `require` and `load` model encourages a culture where module boundaries are relatively soft. Files are loaded, classes and modules are opened or reopened, and **monkey patching** is normal enough that visibility boundaries often function more as convention than hard encapsulation. `require 'foo'` executes `foo.rb` once and remembers it; `load 'foo.rb'` re-executes every time (used for dev workflows). `require_relative` resolves relative to the calling file rather than the load path.

Ruby's `module` keyword declares a namespace and mixin source; `class` declares a class. Both are open — re-declaring `class String` or `module Enumerable` adds methods to the existing object. This is productive in small systems and REPL-heavy workflows, but it weakens the guarantees that tooling and architecture can rely on.

sections.title: Trade-offs and design lessons
sections.content:
Ruby is a useful cautionary reference point for language design. It shows that a language can survive with highly flexible runtime loading, but that such flexibility tends to weaken the compiler and toolchain's ability to reason about program structure in advance.

For a language designer, the lesson is that **monkey-patching and open classes are downstream consequences of the loader model**. If imports are runtime events with no enforced visibility, then any loaded code can reach into and modify any other loaded code's state. Closing this off requires either a stricter import semantics (Rust, Go) or an explicit visibility-and-encapsulation discipline (Ada packages, ML signatures, Swift access controls).
